sectionDescription: Technical reference tables: transponder codes, VHF theoretical range formula, radar and ADS-B coverage, and light signals to aircraft.

categories.title: Miscellaneous Technical
categories.description: Reference tables and technical data for IFR operations.

modules.title: Transponder Codes
modules.summary: Standard SSR transponder codes for different flight rule and airspace combinations in Australian airspace.

content: VFR in Class E or G or OCTA: squawk 1200.
IFR OCTA: squawk 2000.
IFR in Class E or civil flights in CTA: squawk 3000.
Special purpose codes (e.g. 7600 communications failure, 7700 emergency) take precedence over the above.
1200 — VFR in Class E, Class G, or OCTA
2000 — IFR OCTA
3000 — IFR in Class E or civil flights in CTA
7600 — communications failure
7700 — emergency
AIP ENR 1.6 Para 7.1.4
JEPP ATC — ATS Surveillance Services and Procedures 7.1.4

modules.title: VHF Theoretical Maximum Range
modules.summary: Formula for calculating the theoretical maximum range of VHF radio transmissions based on transmitter elevation and receiver height.

content: To determine the theoretical maximum range of VHF transmissions: d = 1.23 × (√Htx + √Hrx), where d = distance in NM, Htx = elevation in feet of transmitter, Hrx = height in feet above ground level of receiver.
1.23 — constant in VHF range formula
d (NM) = 1.23 × (√Htx + √Hrx)
General aviation technical knowledge

modules.title: Radar and ADS-B Coverage
modules.summary: Reference to Airservices Australia radar and ADS-B coverage charts for Australian airspace.

content: Radar and ADS-B coverage in Australian airspace is depicted on charts published by CASA and Airservices Australia. Coverage varies by altitude and geographic location. ADS-B coverage at or above 5000FT is depicted on the Airservices Australia ADS-B 5000FT coverage chart.
Pilots operating IFR must be aware of whether they are within radar or ADS-B coverage, as this affects communications failure procedures and ATC surveillance capability.
5000FT — reference altitude for ADS-B coverage charts
CASA — Chapter 4 Surveillance and ADS-B
Airservices Australia ADS-B 5000ft Coverage Chart

modules.title: Light Signals to Aircraft
modules.summary: ATC light signal meanings for aircraft in flight and on the airport, and acknowledgement procedures.

content: Steady green — in flight: authorised to land if pilot satisfied no collision risk exists; on airport: authorised to take-off if pilot satisfied no collision risk exists.
Steady red — in flight: give way to other aircraft and continue circling; on airport: stop.
Green flashes — in flight: return for landing; on airport: authorised to taxi if pilot satisfied no collision risk exists.
Red flashes — in flight: airport unsafe, do not land; on airport: taxi clear of landing area in use.
White flashes — in flight: no significance; on airport: return to starting point on airport.
Aircraft acknowledgement in flight — by day: rock wings (not expected on base and final); by night: flash landing lights twice or, if not equipped, flash navigation lights twice.
Aircraft acknowledgement on the ground — by day: move ailerons or rudder; by night: flash landing lights twice or, if not equipped, flash navigation lights twice.
AIP ENR 1.5 Para 12
JEPP ATC — Airports and Ground Aids 4
